meta.subject: Physics
meta.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

notes.title: Electron Emission
points: Free electrons in a metal are free to move inside the metal but cannot escape the surface on their own.
Work Function (Φ₀): The minimum amount of energy required by an electron to escape from the metal surface.
Measured in electron-volt (eV). 1 eV = 1.602 x 10^-19 J.
Types of emission: Thermionic (heating), Field (strong electric field), Photoelectric (light irradiation).

notes.title: Photoelectric Effect
points: The phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation of sufficiently high frequency falls on it.
Hertz's Observation: Electric discharge across a spark gap was enhanced when the emitter plate was illuminated by UV light.
Hallwachs' and Lenard's Observation: UV light falling on a negative plate caused current to flow; no current flowed when frequency was below a threshold.

notes.title: Experimental Setup for Photoelectric Effect
notes.caption: Evacuated glass tube with photosensitive plate (C) and collector plate (A).

notes.title: Effect of Intensity on Photocurrent
points: Photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of incident radiation (provided frequency > threshold frequency).
Number of photoelectrons emitted per second is proportional to intensity.

notes.title: Graph: Photocurrent vs Intensity
notes.caption: Straight line passing through origin showing linear relationship.

notes.title: Effect of Potential on Photocurrent
points: Saturation Current: Maximum current when all emitted electrons reach the collector.
Stopping Potential (V₀): The minimum negative (retarding) potential applied to the collector plate for which the photocurrent stops.
Stopping potential is independent of intensity but depends on frequency.

notes.title: Graph: Photocurrent vs Potential (Effect of Intensity)
notes.caption: Different intensities I1, I2, I3 have different saturation currents but same stopping potential.

notes.title: Graph: Photocurrent vs Potential (Effect of Frequency)
notes.caption: Different frequencies v1, v2, v3 have different stopping potentials but same saturation current (for same intensity).

notes.title: Effect of Frequency on Stopping Potential
points: Stopping potential varies linearly with the frequency of incident radiation.
There exists a minimum cut-off frequency (Threshold Frequency, ν₀) below which no emission occurs.

notes.title: Graph: Stopping Potential vs Frequency
notes.caption: Linear graph. Slope = h/e. Intercept on potential axis = -Φ₀/e.

notes.label: Maximum Kinetic Energy
conditions: K_max depends linearly on frequency, not intensity.

notes.title: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation
points: Light consists of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons.
Energy of each photon is E = hν.
Photoelectric emission is a one-to-one collision between a photon and an electron.
Explains instantaneous emission (no time lag).

notes.label: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation
conditions: hν is energy of incident photon.
K_max is maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron.

notes.title: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
points: In interaction with matter, radiation behaves as if it is made up of particles called photons.
All photons of a particular frequency ν have the same energy E=hν and momentum p=hν/c.
Photons are electrically neutral.
Photon mass: Rest mass is zero. Kinetic mass m = E/c².
In a photon-particle collision, total energy and total momentum are conserved, but photon number may not be conserved.

notes.title: Wave Nature of Matter
points: de Broglie Hypothesis: Moving particles of matter should display wave-like properties.
Matter waves are also called de Broglie waves.

notes.label: de Broglie Wavelength
conditions: p is momentum of the particle.
Connects particle characteristic (p) with wave characteristic (λ).

notes.statement: de Broglie Wavelength of Electron accelerated by potential V
notes.valid_when: V is the accelerating potential in volts. m is mass of electron.

notes.title: Davisson and Germer Experiment
points: Verified the wave nature of electrons.
Electrons scattered by a nickel crystal showed a diffraction pattern.
The intensity of scattered electrons showed a strong peak at a specific angle, corresponding to constructive interference.

notes.title: Davisson-Germer Experimental Setup
notes.caption: Electron gun directs beam at Nickel target; moveable detector measures intensity.

notes.title: Polar Graph of Scattering Intensity
notes.caption: Strong peak observed at 54V and 50 degree scattering angle.

notes.label: Bragg's Law
conditions: Used to calculate theoretical wavelength in Davisson-Germer experiment.

formula_sheet.description: Max Kinetic Energy vs Stopping Potential

ncert_golden_lines: The work function of platinum is the highest (5.65 eV) while it is the lowest (2.14 eV) for caesium.
Photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.
The stopping potential is independent of the intensity of the incident radiation.
The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons varies linearly with the frequency of incident radiation.
Light has a dual nature: it behaves as waves in some experiments (interference, diffraction) and as particles in others (photoelectric effect).
The de Broglie wavelength of a photon is the same as the wavelength of electromagnetic radiation of which the photon is a quantum.
